summary: Yesterday's Weather is an estimation principle from Extreme Programming that suggests planning to accomplish as much in the current iteration as was completed in the previous one. Fowler recounts the term's origin from his collaboration with Kent Beck on their XP book. The name derives from a story about an expensive weather prediction system that achieved only marginally better accuracy than simply predicting today's weather will match yesterday's. The post argues that this crude but effective heuristic rivals more sophisticated planning approaches in accuracy.
key_points: Plan to accomplish as much this iteration as you did last iteration
The term originated during Fowler's collaboration with Kent Beck on their Extreme Programming book
Simple heuristics can match the accuracy of far more expensive and complex systems
Sophistication in estimation often adds complexity without proportional accuracy gains
money_quotes: This is the principle that says you'll get as much done today as you got done yesterday.
Somebody then figures out that in this country if you predict today's weather will be the same as yesterday's weather you will be 69.5% accurate.
While Yesterdays Weather is a crude mechanism, it ends up being not significantly less accurate than more sophisticated (i.e. complicated) ways of doing it.
key_insight: Simple estimation based on past performance often matches the accuracy of sophisticated prediction systems while avoiding their complexity and cost.
